content.text: Every database begins with raw data — discrete, recorded facts about entities in the world. Before writing a single SQL query, it helps to understand exactly what data is, how relational databases store and enforce it, and why the choices made at this foundational level reverberate through every aspect of schema design, query performance, and application reliability.
Data (singular: datum, from Latin meaning "a given thing") is the raw material of any information system. In isolation, a value like
carries no inherent meaning. Only when combined with context — a column named
, belonging to a row that describes a specific employee — does it become interpretable. Data is the foundation; information is what emerges from querying, aggregating, and relating data.
In a relational database, data is organized as typed values stored at the intersection of rows and columns inside tables. Each column declares a data type (such as

), and the database engine enforces that every value stored in that column conforms to the declared type. This is fundamentally different from a spreadsheet, where a cell labelled "Salary" will happily hold the text "unknown" next to numeric values, silently breaking any arithmetic downstream.
Data also has a special marker:

. NULL does not mean zero, empty string, or false. It means "the value is unknown or not applicable." This three-valued logic (true, false, unknown) is one of the most important and frequently misunderstood aspects of working with data in SQL.

Every SQL skill — querying, indexing, schema design, transactions, performance tuning — rests on a correct understanding of data and its types. A wrong type choice at schema creation time is extremely costly to fix later: altering a column type on a table with hundreds of millions of rows requires a full table rewrite, locking reads and writes for hours or days on most engines without careful migration tooling.
Misunderstanding NULL causes some of the most subtle and hard-to-find SQL bugs. A query like

is NULL, because NULL compared to anything yields UNKNOWN, which is never true. Applications that don't account for NULL correctly ship with data loss that can persist for months before being noticed.
Type enforcement also directly affects data quality across an entire application. When the database enforces that

, no application bug, bad API call, or bulk import can silently store the string "not yet determined" there. The database becomes the last line of defense for data integrity, and that defense starts with correctly typed columns.
Type safety at the storage layer
— the database rejects invalid values before they can corrupt downstream logic, without any application-layer code
— typed columns allow the engine to build compact, fast B-tree or hash indexes; a numeric primary key index is far smaller than one on unbounded text
— SQL expresses what data to retrieve, not how to iterate over it; the optimizer maps that intent to an execution plan
— all applications share one authoritative copy; no sync issues between distributed files or service caches
— using DECIMAL for money means the database performs exact decimal arithmetic; floating-point types cannot represent most decimal fractions exactly
— typed columns require upfront design decisions; adding a column or changing a type in production is a migration, not a one-line edit
— migrating a column from VARCHAR to DECIMAL on a 500-million-row table requires careful online migration strategies; the mistake is not cheap to fix
— data accumulates without a retention policy; archival, partitioning, and purging strategies must be planned before they become urgent
— not all real-world data fits neatly into typed columns; hierarchical, graph-shaped, or highly variable attribute sets strain the relational model
— three-valued logic requires explicit IS NULL / IS NOT NULL checks; developers from most programming languages expect two-valued boolean logic and are often surprised
Each data type maps to a physical storage format. The database engine serialises values into fixed or variable-length byte sequences on disk and deserialises them during reads. For example, a
is stored as 4 bytes in two's-complement notation, while a
is stored as a length prefix followed by the UTF-8 bytes of the actual string (up to 100 characters, not 100 bytes in multibyte encodings).
Type declarations inform the query planner's decisions. Knowing a column is

(variable width, potentially large) lets the planner select more efficient join and index strategies, estimate row widths for memory budgeting, and push range comparisons down into index scans rather than sequential scans.

NULL is not a value — it is a marker indicating absence or unknownness. Every row in a table stores a NULL bitmap alongside its data, one bit per nullable column, to indicate which column values are absent. Comparisons involving NULL follow three-valued logic:
evaluates to UNKNOWN (not TRUE), which is why
are required instead of equality operators.
is the standard idiom for substituting a fallback when a value may be NULL.
Beyond type matching, databases enforce additional data rules through constraints declared at schema definition time:
— the column must always hold a value; absence is not permitted
— no two rows may share the same value in this column (NULL values are typically exempt and may repeat)
— a boolean expression that must evaluate to TRUE for any inserted or updated row; for example,
— combines NOT NULL and UNIQUE; identifies each row uniquely within the table
— enforces referential integrity; the value in this column must match an existing value in another table's column
The diagram below shows how raw, unorganized facts become structured data inside a relational database, and how SQL queries transform that structured data into actionable information.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing raw facts becoming typed table rows, then being queried to produce information

content.text: Raw facts such as names, salaries, and dates enter the database through INSERT statements. The schema's type declarations and constraints act as a filter: only valid, typed data is persisted. Once stored, SQL SELECT statements, aggregations, and JOINs derive information — averages, counts, summaries — that would be meaningless from a single raw value.
Understanding data and data types applies to every column definition in every table. More specifically, consider these decision points:
) for money, quantities, or any value requiring exact decimal arithmetic. Never use
) for any event time that crosses time zones or must survive DST transitions correctly.
for primary keys on tables expected to exceed ~2 billion rows (INTEGER max: ~2.1 billion). Starting with INTEGER and migrating later is painful.
Reserve NULL for genuine unknowns. If absence is not meaningful in the domain, add NOT NULL and provide a sensible default.
Avoid varchar for everything: VARCHAR(255) for a status column that only ever holds 10 known values is a missed opportunity for a CHECK constraint or an enum type.
Defining a table means specifying, for each column, its name, type, and any constraints. The following example creates an employees table with a representative set of data types and constraints:

CREATE TABLE employees (
    id          BIGINT          PRIMARY KEY GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,
    name        VARCHAR(100)    NOT NULL,
    email       VARCHAR(200)    NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    salary      DECIMAL(12, 2)  NOT NULL CHECK (salary > 0),
    department  VARCHAR(50),
    hired_date  DATE            NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    active      BOOLEAN         NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
);
for the primary key future-proofs against the 2-billion INTEGER ceiling.
is the SQL-standard replacement for vendor-specific SERIAL/AUTO_INCREMENT; the engine assigns the next integer automatically.
stores salary with exactly two decimal places and up to 12 significant digits — sufficient for any realistic salary value while guaranteeing exact arithmetic.
is nullable (no NOT NULL) because a newly hired contractor may not yet be assigned to a department — NULL here is semantically correct.

INSERT INTO employees (name, email, salary, department, hired_date)
VALUES
    ('Alice',      'alice@example.com', 72000.00, 'Engineering', '2023-06-01'),
    ('Bob',        'bob@example.com',   61500.00, 'Marketing',   '2024-01-15'),
    ('Carol',      'carol@example.com', 85000.00, 'Engineering', '2022-09-30'),
    ('David',      'david@example.com', 54000.00,  NULL,         '2024-06-10');

-- Verify stored data and its types
SELECT id, name, salary, department, hired_date
FROM employees
ORDER BY hired_date;

-- NULL-safe check: which employees have no department yet?
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE department IS NULL;

CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id        BIGINT          PRIMARY KEY GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,
    customer_id     BIGINT          NOT NULL,
    status          VARCHAR(20)     NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending'
                                    CHECK (status IN ('pending','paid','shipped','delivered','cancelled')),
    total_amount    DECIMAL(10, 2)  NOT NULL CHECK (total_amount >= 0),
    currency        CHAR(3)         NOT NULL DEFAULT 'USD',
    placed_at       TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    shipped_at      TIMESTAMPTZ,
    notes           TEXT
);

-- Query: revenue from shipped/delivered orders this month
SELECT
    COUNT(*)                           AS order_count,
    SUM(total_amount)                  AS total_revenue,
    ROUND(AVG(total_amount), 2)        AS avg_order_value
FROM orders
WHERE status IN ('shipped', 'delivered')
  AND placed_at >= DATE_TRUNC('month', NOW());
Note the design decisions encoded in the schema:
for ISO 4217 currency codes (always exactly 3 characters),
for freeform notes (unbounded length), and a CHECK constraint on status that prevents any value outside the defined workflow states from being persisted.
A SaaS analytics platform stores billions of event rows. Each event has a fixed set of typed attributes: a
column for flexible per-event properties. Typed columns for the fixed attributes enable a B-tree index on
that makes per-user timeline queries fast even at billions of rows. The JSONB column handles the "long tail" of event-specific properties without requiring an ALTER TABLE for every new event type.

CREATE TABLE events (
    event_id      BIGINT      PRIMARY KEY GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,
    user_id       BIGINT      NOT NULL,
    event_name    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    occurred_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    properties    JSONB
);

CREATE INDEX idx_events_user_time ON events (user_id, occurred_at DESC);

-- How many distinct events per user in the last 30 days?
SELECT
    user_id,
    COUNT(*)             AS event_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT event_name) AS distinct_event_types,
    MIN(occurred_at)     AS first_event,
    MAX(occurred_at)     AS last_event
FROM events
WHERE occurred_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY event_count DESC
LIMIT 20;
A common mistake is using VARCHAR for numeric identifiers or amounts. Text sorting is lexicographic, not numeric: the string '9' sorts after '10' because '9' > '1'.
) cannot represent most decimal fractions exactly. The value 0.10 is not representable in binary floating point, so repeated additions accumulate rounding error:

-- Demonstrates the floating-point precision trap
SELECT 0.1::FLOAT + 0.2::FLOAT;           -- 0.30000000000000004
SELECT 0.1::DECIMAL + 0.2::DECIMAL;       -- 0.3  (exact)

-- Financial column: always use DECIMAL
ALTER TABLE transactions
    ALTER COLUMN amount TYPE DECIMAL(12, 4)
    USING amount::DECIMAL(12, 4);

-- Wrong: = NULL never matches (evaluates to UNKNOWN, not TRUE)
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = NULL;

-- Right: IS NULL
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department IS NULL;

-- Wrong: aggregate ignores NULLs silently — result may mislead
SELECT AVG(commission) FROM sales;   -- NULLs excluded from average denominator

-- Right: decide whether NULL means 0 or genuinely unknown
SELECT AVG(COALESCE(commission, 0)) FROM sales;  -- treat NULL as 0
Storing multiple values in one column (violates 1NF)
Choose the most specific type that fits the domain
: INTEGER for counts, DECIMAL for monetary values, DATE for calendar dates without time, TIMESTAMPTZ for instants in time across time zones.
and allow NULL only when absence is genuinely meaningful in the domain, not just for convenience.
Document what NULL means per column
in comments or a data dictionary; NULL in salary could mean "contractor rate varies" or "data not yet collected" — ambiguity causes bugs.
to encode domain invariants directly in the schema, so they are enforced regardless of which application or migration script writes to the table.
: a status column with a known set of values benefits from a CHECK constraint or an ENUM type, which the engine can validate and compress.
that can be computed from other columns (e.g., a full_name column alongside first_name and last_name) — it will drift out of sync. Use computed columns or views instead.
(created_at, updated_at, and optionally deleted_at for soft-deletes) to every table so record history is traceable without application-level instrumentation.
Data type choices have direct, measurable performance consequences that compound with table size.
Narrower columns mean more rows fit per disk page. A B-tree index on a
column (8 bytes per key) is significantly smaller than one on a
column (16 bytes per key) or an email address
(up to 200 bytes per key). At one billion rows, the difference in index size can be tens of gigabytes, which directly affects whether indexes fit in RAM and thus whether queries are I/O-bound.
are stored identically; the only difference is that VARCHAR adds an engine-enforced length constraint. MySQL/MariaDB treats them differently internally. The practical rule: use
when length is genuinely unbounded (user-authored content), and
when there is a meaningful domain ceiling (email addresses, codes).

PostgreSQL's TOAST (The Oversized-Attribute Storage Technique) mechanism automatically compresses or moves out-of-line values that exceed approximately 2 kB. This means large TEXT or JSONB columns are physically stored in a separate TOAST table and fetched only when queried, keeping the main table's page cache efficient. Querying a column that triggers TOAST fetch for every row has measurable overhead; avoid selecting large columns in wide result sets when only a few are needed.
As data volumes grow into the hundreds of millions of rows, partitioning by a typed column (commonly
) enables the query planner to prune entire partitions from scans. This only works correctly when the partitioning column has the right type — storing dates as
Modern database engines extend the standard SQL type system with platform-specific additions that handle common data patterns more efficiently.
: JSONB (binary JSON with indexing), ARRAY types, UUID, INET/CIDR (IP addresses), TSRANGE/DATERANGE (range types with overlap operators), and ENUM.
: ENUM (stored as small integers), SET, JSON (5.7+), spatial types (POINT, POLYGON).
: uses dynamic typing (type affinity); any column can hold any value type. Useful for embedded use but makes strict type enforcement an application responsibility.
: Flyway and Liquibase manage versioned SQL migration scripts for evolving schemas safely in production. Alembic is the standard for Python/SQLAlchemy projects.
: DBeaver, DataGrip, and pgAdmin provide column-level statistics (min, max, distinct count, null count) to audit existing data quality before or after migrations.
Relational databases are one of several ways to store and retrieve data. The right choice depends on data shape, access patterns, and consistency requirements.
Joins and multi-doc transactions are complex
No querying, no integrity, full scans only
No constraints, error-prone at scale, no concurrent access
No querying by value, limited data modeling
Data validation as a security layer
Constraints at the database layer act as a second line of defense after application validation. If an application bug or a direct database connection bypasses server-side validation, a
constraint on a status column or a

constraint still prevents corrupt data from being written. Parameterized queries (prepared statements) prevent SQL injection by separating SQL code from data values, ensuring user-supplied strings are always treated as data, never as SQL syntax.

Encrypt sensitive columns (PII, payment data) at rest using database-level transparent data encryption (TDE) or application-level encryption before storage. Storing a hash of a password is correct; storing the plaintext is never acceptable.
Use row-level security (PostgreSQL RLS) to enforce per-user or per-tenant data visibility directly in the database, rather than relying entirely on application-layer filtering.
column) over hard-deletes for auditable systems; hard-deleting data that regulators or support teams may need causes compliance and operational incidents.
Schedule regular automated backups with point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled. Test restores on a cadence — discovering a backup is corrupt only during a disaster is too late.

Think of a relational database table as a highly disciplined ledger book. Each column is a headed column in the ledger with a declared unit (dollars, dates, names), and each row is one entry. The ledger refuses to accept an entry that writes text in the "Amount" column or leaves the "Date" field blank when it's mandatory. The raw entries are data; the totals, trends, and reports derived from those entries are information. The discipline that prevents garbage entries is what makes the derived reports trustworthy.
Data is discrete, recorded facts; information is meaning derived from querying and aggregating that data — the two are distinct.
In SQL, every column has a declared type; the database engine enforces type correctness at write time, not at query time.
NULL means unknown or absent, not zero or empty; it follows three-valued logic and requires IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for comparison.
Use DECIMAL for money and exact arithmetic; never use FLOAT or DOUBLE PRECISION for financial values.
Constraints (NOT NULL, CHECK, UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY) encode domain rules in the schema and enforce them regardless of which application writes to the database.
Type choices made at schema creation time are expensive to change at scale — correct decisions upfront pay dividends for the lifetime of the system.
Parameterized queries are the fundamental protection against SQL injection; they ensure user-supplied values are always treated as data, never as SQL syntax.
